title: Global and Local Assignment Files

Global and Local Assignment Files

Global and Local Assignment Files

When you start an application, Uniface builds an internal assignment file from a global assignment file (usys.asn) followed by a local assignment file.

When handling any assignable item for the first time, Uniface scans its internal assignment file, starting with the global assignments and ending with the local assignments, looking for a match. Local assignment settings take precedence over global assignment settings.

Global Assignment File

The global assignment file contains settings that are applicable to all Uniface applications. Every Uniface application looks for global assignments in a file called usys.asn in the Uniface installation directory. Uniface uses the assignments from usys.asn, along with any local assignments that have been located, to create its internal assignment file. The global assignments from this file can be overruled by local assignments.

Note: The method that Uniface uses for assembling its internal assignment file has implications when assignments involving wildcards are used. For more information, see Assignment Settings.

Local Assignment Files

Local assignment files contain settings that are specific to an application. You can specify the local assignment file to be used by an application in one of the following ways:

  • Give the assignment file the same name as the application shell with the .asn extension and store it in the directory where you start the application.

    For example, if there is a file called sales.asn in the directory where you start the application SALES, that file is used as the local assignment file.

  • Start the application using the /asn command line switch, specifying the name of the assignment file to use. For example: 
    sales /asn=myasns
  • On platforms where the application shell must be linked, you can specify the assignment file to be used for the application in the Assignment File property of the application shell.
    Note: An assignment file entered here is not recognized on platforms such as Microsoft Windows, where a single executable is used to start all user applications. This file is usually uniface.exe.

In the last two cases, if you do not specify an extension, .asn is appended to the name provided. The first assignment file encountered from this list is the one used for local assignments.

Related Links
  • /asn

    Specify the assignment file used by the application.
